1.o: The Israelites were convinced that the war against the Canaanites was to be continued; but they were in doubt as to the manner in which it was to be carried on after the death of Joshua. In these respects they inquired of the Lord. God appoints service according to the strength he has given. From those who are most able, most work is expected. Judah was first in dignity, and must be first in duty. Judah's service will not avail unless God give success; but God will not give the success, unless Judah applies to the service. Judah was the most considerable of all the tribes, and Simeon the least; yet Judah begs Simeon's friendship, and prays for aid from him. It becomes Israelites to help one another against Canaanites; and all Christians, even those of different tribes, should strengthen one another. Those who thus help one another in love, have reason to hope that God will graciously help both. Adoni-bezek was taken prisoner. This prince had been a severe tyrant. The Israelites, doubtless under the Divine direction, made him suffer what he had done to others; and his own conscience confessed that he was justly treated as he had treated others. Thus the righteous God sometimes, in his providence, makes the punishment answer the sin.
1.m:
The Israelites knew they needed to keep fighting the Canaanites after Joshua died, but they weren't sure how to do it. So they asked God for guidance. 

God gives people tasks based on their abilities. He expects more from those who can handle more. Judah was the highest-ranking tribe, so they led the way. But Judah couldn’t win without God’s help. And God wouldn’t help if Judah didn’t try hard.

Judah reached out to Simeon, the smallest tribe, asking them to join forces. The Israelites should support each other against their enemies. Likewise, Christians should help each other too, no matter what group they belong to. When believers work together in love, God often helps them succeed.

Adoni-bezek was captured by the Israelites. He used to be a cruel ruler. The Israelites punished him the way he had punished others, likely following God’s plan. His own guilt made him admit that he deserved what he got. Sometimes, God uses events to match punishments to sins.

1.o: The Canaanites had iron chariots; but Israel had God on their side, whose chariots are thousands of angels, Ps 68:17. Yet they suffered their fears to prevail against their faith. About Caleb we read in Jos 15:16–19. The Kenites had settled in the land. Israel let them fix where they pleased, being a quiet, contented people. They that molested none, were molested by none. Blessed are the meek, for they shall inherit the earth.
1.m:
The Canaanites had iron chariots, but Israel had God on their side. God has thousands of angels at his command, as Psalm 68:17 tells us. But instead of trusting God, the Israelites let their fears take over.

We learn about Caleb from Joshua chapter 15, verses 16 through 19. Some Kenites had moved into the land. The Israelites allowed them to stay wherever they wanted because the Kenites were peaceful and content. People who don’t bother others usually aren’t bothered themselves. Those who are gentle will receive the earth as an inheritance.

1.o: The people of Israel were very careless of their duty and interest. Owing to slothfulness and cowardice, they would not be at the pains to complete their conquests. It was also owing to their covetousness: they were willing to let the Canaanites live among them, that they might make advantage of them. They had not the dread and detestation of idolatry they ought to have had. The same unbelief that kept their fathers forty years out of Canaan, kept them now out of the full possession of it. Distrust of the power and promise of God deprived them of advantages, and brought them into troubles. Thus many a believer who begins well is hindered. His graces languish, his lusts revive, Satan plies him with suitable temptations, the world recovers its hold; he brings guilt into his conscience, anguish into his heart, discredit on his character, and reproach on the gospel. Though he may have sharp rebukes, and be so recovered that he does not perish, yet he will have deeply to lament his folly through his remaining days; and upon his dying bed to mourn over the opportunities of glorifying God and serving the church he has lost. We can have no fellowship with the enemies of God within us or around us, but to our hurt; therefore our only wisdom is to maintain unceasing war against them.
1.m:
The people of Israel weren’t careful about doing what they should do. Because they were lazy and scared, they didn’t finish conquering the land. They were also greedy. They let some Canaanites stay because they could use them for money. 

They did not fear or hate idols enough. The same doubt that kept their ancestors from entering Canaan for forty years stopped them now too. Not trusting God’s power and promises made them miss out on blessings and face problems instead.

Many believers start strong but then get sidetracked. Their good qualities weaken, bad habits come back, and Satan tempts them. The world pulls them in again. This causes guilt, sadness, and shame. Even if they get sharp warnings and don’t end up losing everything, they still regret wasting chances to honor God and help others.

We shouldn’t mix with anyone who opposes God inside us or outside us. That only hurts us. Our best choice is to always fight against those enemies.

2.o: It was the great Angel of the covenant, the Word, the Son of God, who spake with Divine authority as Jehovah, and now called them to account for their disobedience. God sets forth what he had done for Israel, and what he had promised. Those who throw off communion with God, and have fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness, know not what they do now, and will have nothing to say for themselves in the day of account shortly. They must expect to suffer for this their folly. Those deceive themselves who expect advantages from friendship with God's enemies. God often makes men's sin their punishment; and thorns and snares are in the way of the froward, who will walk contrary to God. The people wept, crying out against their own folly and ingratitude. They trembled at the word, and not without cause. It is a wonder sinners can ever read the Bible with dry eyes. Had they kept close to God and their duty, no voice but that of singing had been heard in their congregation; but by their sin and folly they made other work for themselves, and nothing is to be heard but the voice of weeping. The worship of God, in its own nature, is joy, praise, and thanksgiving; our sins alone render weeping needful. It is pleasing to see men weep for their sins; but our tears, prayers, and even amendment, cannot atone for sin.
2.m:
It was the great Angel of the covenant, Jesus Christ, speaking with divine authority as Jehovah, who told them they were being disobedient. God reminded them of all He had done for Israel and the promises He had made. People who stop talking to God and start hanging around with those who don’t follow Him don't realize what they're doing. On judgment day, they won't have anything good to say about themselves. 

Those who think they'll get benefits from befriending God’s enemies fool themselves. Sinners often bring trouble on themselves. Thorns and traps await those who go against God. When the people heard this, they cried because they saw their foolishness and lack of gratitude. Their trembling wasn't wrong—they should fear God’s words.

It’s surprising that sinners can read the Bible without feeling upset. If they stayed faithful, only joyful songs would be heard in church. But because of their sins, there was nothing but weeping. Worshiping God naturally brings joy, praise, and thanks. Our sins make us cry instead. Seeing people cry over their sins is good, but tears, prayers, and changes can't erase sin.

2.o: We have a general idea of the course of things in Israel, during the time of the Judges. The nation made themselves as mean and miserable by forsaking God, as they would have been great and happy if they had continued faithful to him. Their punishment answered to the evil they had done. They served the gods of the nations round about them, even the meanest, and God made them serve the princes of the nations round about them, even the meanest. Those who have found God true to his promises, may be sure that he will be as true to his threatenings. He might in justice have abandoned them, but he could not for pity do it. The Lord was with the judges when he raised them up, and so they became saviours. In the days of the greatest distress of the church, there shall be some whom God will find or make fit to help it. The Israelites were not thoroughly reformed; so mad were they upon their idols, and so obstinately bent to backslide. Thus those who have forsaken the good ways of God, which they have once known and professed, commonly grow most daring and desperate in sin, and have their hearts hardened. Their punishment was, that the Canaanites were spared, and so they were beaten with their own rod. Men cherish and indulge their corrupt appetites and passions; therefore God justly leaves them to themselves, under the power of their sins, which will be their ruin. God has told us how deceitful and desperately wicked our hearts are, but we are not willing to believe it, until by making bold with temptation we find it true by sad experience. We need to examine how matters stand with ourselves, and to pray without ceasing, that we may be rooted and grounded in love, and that Christ may dwell in our hearts by faith. Let us declare war against every sin, and follow after holiness all our days.
2.m:
We get an overview of what happened in Israel during the time of the Judges. When the people turned away from God, they became weak and unhappy. But if they had stayed loyal to God, they would have been strong and joyful. 

Their punishment matched the wrongs they did. They worshiped the lesser gods of nearby nations, and God let them be ruled by the weaker leaders of those nations. People who see God keep his promises can trust that he will also keep his warnings. 

God could have left them alone because they deserved it, but he didn’t want to. Instead, God helped the judges he chose to lead. These judges saved the people when times were hard. 

The Israelites weren't fully changed. They still loved their false gods and kept turning away from God’s ways. Once people leave God’s path, they often become more stubborn in their sins. This happens because they stop caring about the truth they knew before. 

As punishment, God allowed the Canaanites to stay around, causing trouble for the Israelites. When people give in to bad desires, God lets them face the consequences of their choices. 

We learn from God that our hearts are tricky and full of sin. But we don’t always believe this until we try to resist temptation and fail. We should check where we stand and ask God to help us stay on the right path. 

Let’s fight against every kind of sin and work toward being holy all our lives.

3.o: As the Israelites were a type of the church on earth, they were not to be idle and slothful. The Lord was pleased to try them by the remains of the devoted nations they spared. Temptations and trials detect the wickedness of the hearts of sinners; and strengthen he graces of believers in their daily conflict with Satan, sin, and this evil world. They must live in this world, but they are not of it, and are forbidden to conform to it. This marks the difference between the followers of Christ and mere professors. The friendship of the world is more fatal than its enmity; the latter can only kill the body, but the former murders many precious souls.
3.m:
Like the Israelites were an example of God’s people on Earth, they weren’t supposed to be lazy. God tested them through the leftover bad influences from the nations they didn't destroy completely. Trials show us our true selves—how sinful we really are—and help believers grow stronger each day as they fight against Satan, sin, and the troubles of this world. 

God’s people must live in this world but not follow its ways. This sets apart those who truly follow Jesus from others who just call themselves Christians. Being friends with the world is worse than being its enemy because while enemies might hurt you physically, worldly friendships can harm your soul much deeper.

3.o: The first judge was Othniel: even in Joshua's time Othniel began to be famous. Soon after Israel's settlement in Canaan their purity began to be corrupted, and their peace disturbed. But affliction makes those cry to God who before would scarcely speak to him. God returned in mercy to them for their deliverance. The Spirit of the Lord came upon Othniel. The Spirit of wisdom and courage to qualify him for the service, and the Spirit of power to excite him to it. He first judged Israel, reproved and reformed them, and then went to war. Let sin at home be conquered, that worst of enemies, then enemies abroad will be more easily dealt with. Thus let Christ be our Judge and Lawgiver, then he will save us.
3.m:
The first judge was Othniel. Even during Joshua’s time, Othniel became well-known. After Israel settled in Canaan, their purity started to decline, and their peace was disrupted. 

When people suffer, they often turn to God. So, God showed mercy by helping them. The Spirit of the Lord came upon Othniel. This gave him wisdom, courage, and strength to do his work. First, he led Israel, correcting and reforming them. Then, he went to battle.

We should deal with sins at home first because that is the toughest enemy. Once we conquer those, dealing with outside problems becomes easier. Just as Othniel helped Israel, Christ can help us if we let him be our Judge and Leader.

3.o: When Israel sins again, God raises up a new oppressor. The Israelites did ill, and the Moabites did worse; yet because God punishes the sins of his own people in this world, Israel is weakened, and Moab strengthened against them. If lesser troubles do not do the work, God will send greater. When Israel prays again, God raises up Ehud. As a judge, or minister of Divine justice, Ehud put to death Eglon, the king of Moab, and thus executed the judgments of God upon him as an enemy to God and Israel. But the law of being subject to principalities and powers in all things lawful, is the rule of our conduct. No such commissions are now given; to pretend to them is to blaspheme God. Notice Ehud's address to Eglon. What message from God but a message of vengeance can a proud rebel expect? Such a message is contained in the word of God; his ministers are boldly to declare it, without fearing the frown, or respecting the persons of sinners. But, blessed be God, they have to deliver a message of mercy and of free salvation; the message of vengeance belongs only to those who neglect the offers of grace. The consequence of this victory was, that the land had rest eighty years. It was a great while for the land to rest; yet what is that to the saints' everlasting rest in the heavenly Canaan.
3.m:
When Israel sinned again, God sent a new oppressor. The Israelites acted badly, and the Moabites were even worse. Yet since God punishes the sins of his people here on earth, Israel became weaker, and Moab grew stronger against them. If small problems don’t fix the issue, God sends bigger ones. 

But when Israel prayed again, God raised up Ehud. As a judge, Ehud killed King Eglon of Moab, carrying out God’s judgment on him as an enemy to God and Israel. We should follow the rule of obeying authorities unless they ask us to do wrong things. Today, we don't get special permission to act like Ehud did. To claim such power would be to insult God.

Notice how Ehud spoke to Eglon. A proud rebel like Eglon could only expect a message of punishment from God. This kind of message is found in the Bible. Ministers should speak it clearly without fear or favoritism. Thankfully, today’s message is one of mercy and free salvation. Punishment comes only if someone rejects God’s offer of forgiveness.

After this victory, the land enjoyed peace for eighty years. That was a long time of rest, but eternal rest in heaven is far better.

3.o: The side of the country which lay south-west, was infested by the Philistines. God raised up Shamgar to deliver them; having neither sword nor spear, he took an ox-goad, the instrument next at hand. God can make those serviceable to his glory and to his church's good, whose birth, education, and employment, are mean and obscure. It is no matter what the weapon is, if God directs and strengthens the arm. Often he works by unlikely means, that the excellency of the power may appear to be of God.
3.m:
The area southwest of their land had problems with the Philistines. God chose Shamgar to help them. Since Shamgar didn't have a sword or spear, he used an ox goad, the tool closest to him. 

God can use anyone—even someone born poor and unknown—to do great things for His glory and the good of His people. What matters isn’t the weapon, but whether God guides and strengthens the person using it. 

Often, God uses surprising ways so that everyone sees that all the power comes from Him.

4.o: The land had rest for eighty years, which should have confirmed them in their religion; but it made them secure, and indulge their lusts. Thus the prosperity of fools destroys them. Jabin and his general Sisera, mightily oppressed Israel. This enemy was nearer than any of the former. Israel cried unto the Lord, when distress drove them to him, and they saw no other way of relief. Those who slight God in prosperity, will find themselves under a necessity of seeking him in trouble.
4.m:
The land had peace for eighty years, which should have helped them stay faithful. But instead, it made them careless and gave in to their desires. So, the success of foolish people often leads to their downfall.

Jabin and his commander Sisera were very cruel to Israel. This enemy was closer than any before. When Israel was in trouble, they called out to the Lord because they saw no other way to get help. People who ignore God during good times will need to turn to Him when things go wrong.

4.o: Deborah was a prophetess; one instructed in Divine knowledge by the inspiration of the Spirit of God. She judged Israel as God's mouth to them; correcting abuses, and redressing grievances. By God's direction, she ordered Barak to raise an army, and engage Jabin's forces. Barak insisted much upon her presence. Deborah promised to go with him. She would not send him where she would not go herself. Those who in God's name call others to their duty, should be ready to assist them in it. Barak values the satisfaction of his mind, and the good success of his enterprise, more than mere honour.
4.m:
Deborah was a prophetess. This means she knew things from God and shared His messages. 

She acted as God’s representative for the people of Israel. She corrected wrongdoings and helped solve problems.

God told her to tell Barak to gather an army and fight against Jabin’s troops. 

Barak really wanted Deborah to join him on the battlefield. Deborah agreed to go if he went too. She wouldn’t ask him to do anything she wasn’t willing to do herself.

People who encourage others to follow God’s commands should help those people carry out those tasks. 

Barak cared more about doing what was right and succeeding than just getting credit for it.

4.o: Siser's confidence was chiefly in his chariots. But if we have ground to hope that God goes before us, we may go on with courage and cheerfulness. Be not dismayed at the difficulties thou meetest with in resisting Satan, in serving God, or suffering for him; for is not the Lord gone before thee? Follow him then fully. Barak went down, though upon the plain the iron chariots would have advantage against him: he quitted the mountain in dependence on the Divine power; for in the Lord alone is the salvation of his people, Jer 3:23. He was not deceived in his confidence. When God goes before us in our spiritual conflicts, we must bestir ourselves; and when, by his grace, he gives us some success against the enemies of our souls, we must improve it by watchfulness and resolution.
4.m:
Sisera trusted mostly in his chariots. But if we believe that God is leading us, we can be brave and happy as we move forward. Don't be afraid of the challenges you face while fighting against evil, serving God, or suffering for Him. After all, hasn’t the Lord already gone ahead of you? So follow Him wholeheartedly.

Barak went down from the mountains even though the enemy’s iron chariots had an advantage on flat land. He left the safety of the high ground because he relied on God’s strength. For only in the Lord do His people find rescue, as Jeremiah 3:23 tells us. 

His trust wasn’t misplaced. When God leads us in battles over sin, we should act boldly. And once God helps us gain some victory through His grace, we need to stay alert and determined.

4.o: Sisera's chariots had been his pride and his confidence. Thus are those disappointed who rest on the creature; like a broken reed, it not only breaks under them, but pierces them with many sorrows. The idol may quickly become a burden, Isa 46:1; what we were sick for, God can make us sick of. It is probable that Jael really intended kindness to Sisera; but by a Divine impulse she was afterwards led to consider him as the determined enemy of the Lord and of his people, and to destroy him. All our connexions with God's enemies must be broken off, if we would have the Lord for our God, and his people for our people. He that had thought to have destroyed Israel with his many iron chariots, is himself destroyed with one iron nail. Thus the weak things of the world confound the mighty. The Israelites would have prevented much mischief, if they had sooner destroyed the Canaanites, as God commanded and enabled them: but better be wise late, and buy wisdom by experience, than never be wise.
4.m:
Sisera’s chariots gave him pride and confidence. Those who trust in worldly things get let down because those things break just like a broken reed, which hurts whoever depends on it. An idol can turn against you quickly, becoming a heavy burden. What once brought joy can now bring sickness. 

Jael likely meant to show kindness to Sisera at first, but then felt guided by God to see him as an enemy of God and His people. We need to end all ties with God’s enemies if we want Him to be our God and His people to be ours. Sisera tried to crush Israel with his powerful war machines, yet he ended up being killed by a single nail. This shows how the weak can defeat the strong.

The Israelites could have stopped more harm earlier by following God’s command to destroy the Canaanites. Better to learn from mistakes later than never learn at all.

5.o: No time should be lost in returning thanks to the Lord for his mercies; for our praises are most acceptable, pleasant, and profitable, when they flow from a full heart. By this, love and gratitude would be more excited and more deeply fixed in the hearts of believers; the events would be more known and longer remembered. Whatever Deborah, Barak, or the army had done, the Lord must have all the praise. The will, the power, and the success were all from Him.
5.m:
We shouldn't wait to thank the Lord for His kindness. Our praises are best when they come from a grateful heart. This helps us feel more love and thankfulness. It also helps us remember what God has done for a long time. 

Whatever Deborah, Barak, or the soldiers did, all the credit goes to the Lord. He gave them the desire, the strength, and the victory.

5.o: Deborah describes the distressed state of Israel under the tyranny of Jabin, that their salvation might appear more gracious. She shows what brought this misery upon them. It was their idolatry. They chose new gods, with new names. But under all these images, Satan was worshipped. Deborah was a mother to Israel, by diligently promoting the salvation of their souls. She calls on those who shared the advantages of this great salvation, to offer up thanks to God for it. Let such as are restored, not only to their liberty as other Israelites, but to their rank, speak God's praises. This is the Lord's doing. In these acts of his, justice was executed on his enemies. In times of persecution, God's ordinances, the walls of salvation, whence the waters of life are drawn, are resorted to at the hazard of the lives of those who attend them. At all times Satan will endeavour to hinder the believer from drawing near to the throne of grace. Notice God's kindness to his trembling people. It is the glory of God to protect those who are most exposed, and to help the weakest. Let us notice the benefit we have from the public peace, the inhabitants of villages especially, and give God the praise.
5.m:
Deborah explains how Israel suffered because of the cruel rule of Jabin. She wants everyone to see how kind God was to save them. She points out that their troubles started because they worshiped idols. They picked new gods with different names, but really they were just worshipping Satan.

Deborah cared about Israel’s well-being. She worked hard to help people find true salvation. She asks those who benefited from this rescue to thank God. People who regained both freedom and their former positions should praise God too.

God did all this. He punished His enemies while showing mercy to His people. During tough times, believers risked their lives to follow God’s teachings. The devil always tries to stop people from praying to God. 

Notice how kindly God protected those who were scared. God loves to help the weak and defenseless. We should be thankful for the safety and peace in our communities, especially in small towns. Give God credit for all this.

5.o: Deborah called on her own soul to be in earnest. He that will set the hearts of other men on fire with the love of Christ, must himself burn with love. Praising God is a work we should awake to, and awake ourselves unto. She notices who fought against Israel, who fought for them, and who kept away. Who fought against them. They were obstinate enemies to God's people, therefore the more dangerous. Who fought for them. The several tribes that helped are here spoken of with honour; for though God is above all to be glorified, those who are employed must have their due praise, to encourage others. But the whole creation is at war with those to whom God is an enemy. The river of Kishon fought against their enemies. At most times it was shallow, yet now, probably by the great rain that fell, it was so swelled, and the stream so deep and strong, that those who attempted to pass, were drowned. Deborah's own soul fought against them. When the soul is employed in holy exercises, and heart-work is made of them, through the grace of God, the strength of our spiritual enemies will be trodden down, and will fall before us. She observes who kept away, and did not side with Israel, as might have been expected. Thus many are kept from doing their duty by the fear of trouble, the love of ease, and undue affection to their worldly business and advantage. Narrow, selfish spirits care not what becomes of God's church, so that they can but get, keep, and save money. All seek their own, Php 2:21. A little will serve those for a pretence to stay at home, who have no mind to engage in needful services, because there is difficulty and danger in them. But we cannot keep away from the contest between the Lord and his enemies; and if we do not actively endeavour to promote his cause in this wicked world, we shall fall under the curse against the workers of iniquity. Though He needs no human help, yet he is pleased to accept the services of those who improve their talents to advance his cause. He requires every man to do so.
5.m:
Deborah urged herself to be serious. To inspire others with love for Christ, one must feel that love deeply themselves. We should wake up and energize ourselves to praise God. 

She noted who fought against Israel, who supported them, and who stayed out. Those fighting against them were stubborn foes, making them very dangerous. 

Those who fought for Israel are mentioned with honor. While God deserves all glory, those who carry out His work deserve recognition too. After all, the entire universe fights against those God opposes. 

The River Kishon fought against their enemies. Usually shallow, it likely flooded after heavy rains, drowning those trying to cross. 

Deborah’s own spirit battled against their foes. When our souls focus on holy tasks, with God’s help, we can defeat our spiritual enemies. 

Some didn’t join Israel despite expectations. Fear of hardship, desire for comfort, and concern for personal gain held them back. Self-centered people often don’t care about God’s church, focusing only on money. 

Small excuses prevent many from serving, even when needed. Yet we can't avoid siding with God or against evil. If we don’t support His cause in this sinful world, we face judgment. 

Though God doesn’t need help, He accepts service from those using their gifts to further His mission. Each person has a role to play.

5.o: Jael had a special blessing. Those whose lot is cast in the tent, in a low and narrow sphere, if they serve God according to the powers he has given them, shall not lose their reward. The mother of Sisera looked for his return, not in the least fearing his success. Let us take heed of indulging eager desires towards any temporal good, particularly toward that which cherishes vain-glory, for that was what she here doted on. What a picture does she present of an ungodly and sensual heart! How shameful and childish these wishes of an aged mother and her attendants for her son! And thus does God often bring ruin on his enemies when they are most puffed up. Deborah concludes with a prayer to God for the destruction of all his foes, and for the comfort of all his friends. Such shall be the honour, and joy of all who love God in sincerity, they shall shine for ever as the sun in the firmament.
5.m:
Jael received a special blessing. People who do God’s work even in small ways will get their reward. 

Sisera’s mom waited for him to come home, hoping everything went well. We should watch out for wanting earthly things too much, especially ones that feed pride. That’s what she cared about.

Her hopes show how selfish and focused on worldly things an ungodly person can be. It’s embarrassing and silly for an older woman and those around her to wish so strongly for her son’s glory.

God often brings down His enemies when they’re feeling really proud. 

Deborah ends by praying that God would destroy all His enemies and give comfort to all His friends. This honor and joy belong to everyone who truly loves God—they’ll shine forever like the sun.

6.o: Israel's sin was renewed, and Israel's troubles were repeated. Let all that sin expect to suffer. The Israelites hid themselves in dens and caves; such was the effect of a guilty conscience. Sin dispirits men. The invaders left no food for Israel, except what was taken into the caves. They prepared that for Baal with which God should have been served, now God justly sends an enemy to take it away in the season thereof.
6.m:
Israel kept sinning, so their problems started again. When people do wrong, they often face trouble.

The Israelites hid in caves because of guilt. A bad conscience makes people feel weak.

The enemies took almost everything from Israel, leaving only what was hidden in caves. 

They used things meant for God to worship false gods instead. Now God lets those enemies take away their crops.

6.o: They cried to God for a deliverer, and he sent them a prophet to teach them. When God furnishes a land with faithful ministers, it is a token that he has mercy in store for it. He charges them with rebellion against the Lord; he intends to bring them to repentance. Repentance is real when the sinfulness of sin, as disobedience to God, is chiefly lamented.
6.m: They asked God for help, and he sent them a teacher. When God gives a place good leaders who teach people about him, it shows he wants to be kind to those people. The teacher told them they had been rebellious toward God. He wanted them to feel sorry for what they did wrong. People truly feel sorry when they are sad mainly because they disobeyed God.

6.o: Gideon was a man of a brave, active spirit, yet in obscurity through the times: he is here stirred up to undertake something great. It was very sure that the Lord was with him, when his Angel was with him. Gideon was weak in faith, which made it hard to reconcile the assurances of the presence of God with the distress to which Israel was brought. The Angel answered his objections. He told him to appear and act as Israel's deliverer, there needed no more. Bishop Hall says, While God calls Gideon valiant, he makes him so. God delights to advance the humble. Gideon desires to have his faith confirmed. Now, under the influences of the Spirit, we are not to expect signs before our eyes such as Gideon here desired, but must earnestly pray to God, that if we have found grace in his sight, he would show us a sign in our heart, by the powerful working of his Spirit there, The Angel turned the meat into an offering made by fire; showing that he was not a man who needed meat, but the Son of God, who was to be served and honoured by sacrifice, and who in the fulness of time was to make himself a sacrifice. Hereby a sign was given to Gideon, that he had found grace in God's sight. Ever since man has by sin exposed himself to God's wrath and curse, a message from heaven has been a terror to him, as he scarcely dares to expect good tidings thence. In this world, it is very awful to have any converse with that world of spirits to which we are so much strangers. Gideon's courage failed him. But God spoke peace to him.
6.m:
Gideon was brave and active, but unknown during troubled times. Here, he was encouraged to do something important. When God’s angel was with him, God surely was too. Gideon doubted because he couldn’t see how God could help them while Israel suffered. 

The angel answered Gideon’s doubts. He told Gideon to lead Israel and said that was enough. A bishop noted that when God called Gideon brave, God made him brave. God likes to lift up those who are humble.

Gideon wanted proof. Under God’s guidance, we don't need visible signs like Gideon asked for. We should ask God to give us signs within our hearts, through His Spirit at work inside us.

The angel changed Gideon’s food into an offering. This showed that the angel wasn’t just a person but the Son of God, someone who didn’t need food but was to be honored with sacrifices. This proved to Gideon that God favored him.

Since people sinned against God, messages from heaven often scared them. Talking with spiritual beings feels strange and scary. Gideon felt afraid. But God reassured him and gave him peace.

6.o: See the power of God's grace, that he could raise up a reformer; and the kindness of his grace, that he would raise up a deliverer, out of the family of a leader in idolatry. Gideon must not think it enough not to worship at that altar; he must throw it down, and offer sacrifice on another. It was needful he should make peace with God, before he made war on Midian. Till sin be pardoned through the great Sacrifice, no good is to be expected. God, who has all hearts in his hands, influenced Joash to appear for his son against the advocates for Baal, though he had joined formerly in the worship of Baal. Let us do our duty, and trust God with our safety. Here is a challenge to Baal, to do either good or evil; the result convinced his worshippers of their folly, in praying to one to help them that could not avenge himself.
6.m:
See the power of God’s grace that He raised up a reformer, and His kindness in raising up a deliverer from the family of someone who led in worshipping idols. Gideon shouldn’t just stop worshipping at that altar; he needs to tear it down and offer sacrifices at another one. 

He needed to make peace with God first, before fighting against Midian. Until sins are forgiven by the great Sacrifice, we cannot expect anything good. 

God, who controls everyone’s heart, encouraged Joash to stand up for his son against those who supported Baal, even if Joash had once worshipped Baal too. We should do what is right and leave our safety to God.

There was a challenge to Baal to show some action, whether good or bad. The outcome showed that people were foolish for praying to an idol that couldn’t even defend itself.

6.o: These signs are truly miraculous, and very significant. Gideon and his men were going to fight the Midianites; could God distinguish between a small fleece of Israel, and the vast floor of Midian? Gideon is made to know that God could do so. Is Gideon desirous that the dew of Divine grace might come down upon himself in particular? He sees the fleece wet with dew to assure him of it. Does he desire that God will be as the dew to all Israel? Behold, all the ground is wet. What cause we sinners of the Gentiles have, to bless the Lord that the dew of heavenly blessings, once confined to Israel, is now sent to all the inhabitants of the earth! Yet still the means of grace are in different measures, according to the purposes of God. In the same congregation, one man's soul is like Gideon's moistened fleece, another like the dry ground.
6.m: These signs are really amazing and important. Gideon was about to fight against the Midianites. Could God tell apart a tiny piece of wool from Israel and a huge pile of grain from Midian? God showed Gideon that he could do just that. Did Gideon want God’s blessing to fall on him alone? The wool was soaked with dew to show him it would happen. Did he want God’s favor for all of Israel? The whole area was covered in dew. We non-Jewish people should thank God that the dew of heaven, which used to only go to Israel, now goes to everyone around the world. But even today, some people get more help than others because of what God plans. At church, one person may feel blessed like Gideon’s wet wool, while another feels left out like the dry ground.

7.o: God provides that the praise of victory may be wholly to himself, by appointing only three hundred men to be employed. Activity and prudence go with dependence upon God for help in our lawful undertakings. When the Lord sees that men would overlook him, and through unbelief, would shrink from perilous services, or that through pride they would vaunt themselves against him, he will set them aside, and do his work by other instruments. Pretences will be found by many, for deserting the cause and escaping the cross. But though a religious society may thus be made fewer in numbers, yet it will gain as to purity, and may expect an increased blessing from the Lord. God chooses to employ such as are not only well affected, but zealously affected in a good thing. They grudged not at the liberty of the others who were dismissed. In doing the duties required by God, we must not regard the forwardness or backwardness of others, nor what they do, but what God looks for at our hands. He is a rare person who can endure that others should excel him in gifts or blessings, or in liberty; so that we may say, it is by the special grace of God that we regard what God says to us, and not look to men what they do.
7.m:
God makes sure all the glory goes to Him by choosing just three hundred soldiers. This shows that being active, smart, and relying on God works together.

When God sees people ignoring Him or trying to take credit for His work, He sets them aside and uses someone else instead. Many find excuses to avoid hard tasks or leave risky jobs.

Even if a group gets smaller because some people drop out, it becomes purer and receives more blessings from God. 

God likes to use those who are not just supportive but really passionate about doing good things. The ones left behind didn’t mind letting others go home. 

We shouldn't care whether others step up or back down. We need to focus on what God expects from us. It’s unusual for someone to be okay with others having better gifts or freedoms than them. So we can thank God for helping us listen to what He says rather than worrying about what others do.

7.o: The dream seemed to have little meaning in it; but the interpretation evidently proved the whole to be from the Lord, and discovered that the name of Gideon had filled the Midianites with terror. Gideon took this as a sure pledge of success; without delay he worshipped and praised God, and returned with confidence to his three hundred men. Wherever we are, we may speak to God, and worship him. God must have the praise of that which encourages our faith. And his providence must be acknowledged in events, though small and seemingly accidental.
7.m:
The dream didn’t seem to mean much at first, but the explanation showed that it was really from God. It also showed that the Midianites were scared of Gideon. Gideon felt sure he would win because of this. So he quickly thanked and praised God, then went back to his three hundred men with confidence. 

No matter where we are, we can talk to God and praise him. We should give God credit for things that strengthen our faith. Even small events that seem random should be seen as part of God’s care for us.

7.o: This method of defeating the Midianites may be alluded to, as exemplifying the destruction of the devil's kingdom in the world, by the preaching of the everlasting gospel, the sounding that trumpet, and the holding forth that light out of earthen vessels, for such are the ministers of the gospel, 2Co 4:6, 7. God chose the foolish things of the world to confound the wise, a barley-cake to overthrow the tents of Midian, that the excellency of the power might be of God only. The gospel is a sword, not in the hand, but in the mouth: the sword of the Lord and of Gideon; of God and Jesus Christ, of Him that sits on the throne and the Lamb. The wicked are often led to avenge the cause of God upon each other, under the power of their delusions, and the fury of their passions. See also how God often makes the enemies of the church instruments to destroy one another; it is a pity that the church's friends should ever act like them.
7.m:
This way of defeating the Midianites could be compared to how the devil’s kingdom will be destroyed through the preaching of the everlasting gospel. This is like sounding a trumpet and holding up a light from clay jars, just as the apostle Paul described in 2 Corinthians 4:6-7. 

God chose weak things to defeat strong ones—like using a barley cake to bring down the tents of Midian. He did this so that people would see His power at work. The gospel is a weapon, but it is spoken rather than held in a hand—it’s the sword of the Lord and Gideon, or the sword of God and Jesus Christ, who sit on the throne with the Lamb.

Often, evil people end up punishing each other because they’re deceived and driven by anger. And sometimes, God uses those who oppose the church to fight against each other. It’s sad when the church’s supporters act like its enemies.

7.o: Two chief commanders of the host of Midian were taken and slain by the men of Ephraim. It were to be wished that we all did as these did, and that where help is needed, that it were willingly and readily performed by another. And that if there were any excellent and profitable matter begun, we were willing to have fellow-labourers to the finishing and perfecting the same, and not, as often, hinder one another.
7.m:
Two important leaders from the army of Midian were captured and killed by the people of Ephraim. 

It would be good if everyone helped out like they did. When someone needs assistance, others should be happy to lend a hand. We should want to finish tasks together rather than get in each other’s way.

8.o: Those who will not attempt or venture any thing in the cause of God, will be the most ready to censure and quarrel with such as are of a more zealous and enterprising spirit. And those who are the most backward to difficult services, will be the most angry not to have the credit of them. Gideon stands here as a great example of self-denial; and shows us that envy is best removed by humility. The Ephraimites had given vent to their passion in very wrong freedom of speech, a certain sign of a weak cause: reason runs low when chiding flies high.
8.m:
People who won’t do anything for God’s sake are quick to criticize those who are more eager to serve. Those who avoid hard work get upset if others take the credit. 

Gideon sets an example of being humble, which helps stop jealousy. The people from Ephraim showed their anger by speaking rudely, which means they didn’t have much of a good argument. When someone starts yelling, it usually means they don’t have strong reasons.

8.o: Gideon's men were faint, yet pursuing; fatigued with what they had done, yet eager to do more against their enemies. It is many a time the true Christian's case, fainting, and yet pursuing. The world knows but little of the persevering and successful struggle the real believer maintains with his sinful heart. But he betakes himself to that Divine strength, in the faith of which he began his conflict, and by the supply of which alone he can finish it in triumph.
8.m:
Gideon’s soldiers were tired but still chasing after their enemies. They were worn out from all they had already done, yet they wanted to keep going. 

It often happens with true Christians too. They feel weak but keep pushing forward. Most people don’t see the hard battles believers fight inside themselves. 

But the believer turns to God’s power. This is the same power they relied on at first. Only by using this power can they win their battle in the end.

8.o: The active servants of the Lord meet with more dangerous opposition from false professors than from open enemies; but they must not care for the behaviour of those who are Israelites in name, but Midianites in heart. They must pursue the enemies of their souls, and of the cause of God, though they are ready to faint through inward conflicts and outward hardships. And they shall be enabled to persevere. The less men help, and the more they seek to hinder, the more will the Lord assist. Gideon's warning being slighted, the punishment was just. Many are taught with the briers and thorns of affliction, who would not learn otherwise.
8.m:
The servants of the Lord often face greater danger from fake believers than from open enemies. But they should not worry about people who call themselves followers but act against them. 

They need to fight against the real enemies of their soul and of God’s work, even if they feel worn out by inner struggles and outer troubles. 

God helps them keep going, especially when others don’t help or try to stop them. 

When Gideon warned his people and they ignored him, the punishment came as it should. 

Sometimes, God teaches people using the hard lessons of suffering because they won't listen any other way.

8.o: The kings of Midian must be reckoned with. As they confessed themselves guilty of murder, Gideon acted as the avenger of blood, being the next of kin to the persons slain. Little did they think to have heard of this so long after; but murder seldom goes unpunished in this life. Sins long forgotten by man, must be accounted for to God. What poor consolation in death from the hope of suffering less pain, and of dying with less disgrace than some others! yet many are more anxious on these accounts, than concerning the future judgment, and what will follow.
8.m:
The kings of Midian needed to face consequences. Since they admitted to killing people, Gideon took action as the family member who had to punish them. They probably didn't expect to hear about this so long after. But murder rarely goes unpunished in this life. Even if we forget our sins, we still have to answer to God for them. 

It’s little comfort at death to hope for less pain or less shame compared to others. Yet, many worry more about such things than about facing God’s judgment later.

8.o: Gideon refused the government the people offered him. No good man can be pleased with any honour done to himself, which belongs only to God. Gideon thought to keep up the remembrance of this victory by an ephod, made of the choicest of the spoils. But probably this ephod had, as usual, a teraphim annexed to it, and Gideon intended this for an oracle to be consulted. Many are led into false ways by one false step of a good man. It became a snare to Gideon himself, and it proved the ruin of the family. How soon will ornaments which feed the lust of the eye, and form the pride of life, as well as tend to the indulgences of the flesh, bring shame on those who are fond of them!
8.m:
Gideon turned down the offer to become their leader. A good person doesn’t enjoy being given honor that should go to God alone. 

Gideon wanted to remember his big win by making an ephod from some of the best things he captured. This ephod likely included a teraphim, which was used as an oracle. One wrong move by a good person often leads others astray. The ephod ended up causing trouble for Gideon and even ruined parts of his family.

Things that look nice and fancy, like jewelry, can lead us into trouble if we’re too proud of them. They might end up embarrassing us instead.

8.o: As soon as Gideon was dead, who kept the people to the worship of the God of Israel, they found themselves under no restraint; then they went after Baalim, and showed no kindness to the family of Gideon. No wonder if those who forget their God, forget their friends. Yet conscious of our own ingratitude to the Lord, and observing that of mankind in general, we should learn to be patient under any unkind returns we meet with for our poor services, and resolve, after the Divine example, not to be overcome of evil, but to overcome evil with good.
8.m:
Right after Gideon died, who had kept the people worshipping the God of Israel, they stopped following his rules. They started worshipping other gods called Baalim and were mean to Gideon’s family.

It’s not surprising that people who forget their God also forget to be kind to their friends. 

Knowing how ungrateful we can be to the Lord, and seeing how others often act unfairly toward us, we should learn to be patient. We should deal kindly with everyone, just like God does. Even when people treat us badly, we should try to do good things instead of reacting poorly.

9.o: The men of Shechem chose Abimelech king. God was not consulted whether they should have any king, much less who it should be. If parents could see what their children would do, and what they are to suffer, their joy in them often would be turned into sorrow: we may be thankful that we cannot know what shall happen. Above all, we should fear and watch against sin; for our evil conduct may produce fatal effects upon our families, after we are in our graves.
9.m:
The people of Shechem made Abimelech their king. They did not ask God if they should have a king or who should be king. 

If parents knew what their children would do and what they would go through, they might feel sad instead of happy. We don't know what will happen, so we can be glad about that.

Most importantly, we need to be careful and avoid sin. Our bad choices can hurt our family even long after we're gone.

9.o: There was no occasion for the trees to choose a king, they are all the trees of the Lord which he has planted. Nor was there any occasion for Israel to set a king over them, for the Lord was their King. Those who bear fruit for the public good, are justly respected and honoured by all that are wise, more than those who merely make a figure. All these fruit-trees gave much the same reason for their refusal to be promoted over the trees; or, as the margin reads it, to go up and down for the trees. To rule, involves a man in a great deal both of toil and care. Those who are preferred to public trust and power, must forego all private interests and advantages, for the good of others. And those advanced to honour and dignity, are in great danger of losing their fruitfulness. For which reason, they that desire to do good, are afraid of being too great. Jotham compares Abimelech to the bramble or thistle, a worthless plant, whose end is to be burned. Such a one was Abimelech.
9.m:
There was no need for the trees to pick a king because God had already planted them all. There was also no need for Israel to have a king since the Lord was their King. People respect and honor those who help everyone, more than those who only look important.

All the fruit trees refused to be chosen as leaders. The trees said they didn’t want to travel around managing other trees. Being in charge means lots of hard work and worry. When someone gets an important job, they often have to give up personal benefits to help others.

People who get high positions risk losing what makes them valuable. That’s why people who want to do good things don't usually seek big roles. Jotham compared Abimelech to a useless bramble bush that will be burned. Abimelech was like that—worthless and dangerous.

9.o: Abimelech is seated in the throne his father refused. But how long does this glory last? Stay but three years, and see the bramble withered and burned. The prosperity of the wicked is short and fickle. The Shechemites are plagued by no other hand than Abimelech's. They raised him unjustly to the throne; they first feel the weight of his sceptre.
9.m:
Abimelech sits on the throne that his father declined. But how long will this glory last? Wait just three years, and you'll see the thorny bush wilted and burned. The good fortune of bad people doesn't last long.

The people of Shechem suffer at the hands of Abimelech. They put him on the throne unfairly, so now they're the ones who feel the heavy end of his power.

9.o: Abimelech intended to punish the Schechemites for slighting him now, but God punished them for their serving him formerly in the murder of Gideon's sons. When God uses men as instruments in his hand to do his work, he means one thing, and they another. That, which they hoped would have been for their welfare, proved a snare and a trap, as those will certainly find, who run to idols for shelter; such will prove a refuge of lies. (Jdg 9:50-57)
9.m:
Abimelech wanted to punish the people of Shechem because they were not respecting him now. But God was punishing them for helping Abimelech kill Gideon’s sons before. 

When God uses people to do his work, he has different plans than what they think. What they hoped would help them turned out to be a trap instead. People who turn to false gods for protection will always end up in trouble. (Jdg 9:50-57)

9.o: The Shechemites were ruined by Abimelech; now he is reckoned with, who was their leader in villany. Evil pursues sinners, and sometimes overtakes them, when not only at ease, but triumphant. Though wickedness may prosper a while, it will not prosper always. The history of mankind, if truly told, would greatly resemble that of this chapter. The records of what are called splendid events present to us such contests for power. Such scenes, though praised of men, fully explain the Scripture doctrine of the deceitfulness and desperate wickedness of the human heart, the force of men's lust, and the effect of Satan's influence. Lord, thou has given us thy word of truth and righteousness, O pour upon us thy spirit of purity, peace, and love, and write thy holy law in our hearts.
9.m:
The people of Shechem were destroyed by Abimelech. Now he is being punished too, since he led them to do evil things. Sin often catches up with people, even when they feel safe and proud. Wickedness might seem successful for a time, but it won't last forever. 

If we look closely at human history, it looks a lot like what happens in this story. We see stories about powerful people fighting each other, which shows just how selfish and sinful humans can be. These tales prove what the Bible teaches about how greedy and evil people can be, and how much they're influenced by Satan.

Lord, you have given us your true and righteous Word. Please fill us with your Spirit of purity, peace, and love. Help us keep your holy laws written in our hearts.

10.o: Quiet and peaceable reigns, though the best to live in, yield least variety of matter to be spoken of. Such were the days of Tola and Jair. They were humble, active, and useful men, rulers appointed of God.
10.m: Quiet and peaceful times, even if they are the best to live through, don't provide much to talk about. That was true during the time of Tola and Jair. They were modest, hardworking, and helpful leaders chosen by God.

10.o: Now the threatening was fulfilled, that the Israelites should have no power to stand before their enemies, Le 26:17, 37. By their evil ways and their evil doings they procured this to themselves.
10.m: The warning came true that the Israelites would not be able to fight against their enemies, as Leviticus 26:17 and 37 say. They brought this upon themselves because of their bad actions and wrong choices.

10.o: God is able to multiply men's punishments according to the numbers of their sins and idols. But there is hope when sinners cry to the Lord for help, and lament their ungodliness as well as their more open transgressions. It is necessary, in true repentance, that there be a full conviction that those things cannot help us which we have set in competition with God. They acknowledged what they deserved, yet prayed to God not to deal with them according to their deserts. We must submit to God's justice, with a hope in his mercy. True repentance is not only for sin, but from sin. As the disobedience and misery of a child are a grief to a tender father, so the provocations of God's people are a grief to him. From him mercy never can be sought in vain. Let then the trembling sinner, and the almost despairing backslider, cease from debating about God's secret purposes, or from expecting to find hope from former experiences. Let them cast themselves on the mercy of God our Saviour, humble themselves under his hand, seek deliverance from the powers of darkness, separate themselves from sin, and from occasions of it, use the means of grace diligently, and wait the Lord's time, and so they shall certainly rejoice in his mercy.
10.m:
God can punish people based on how many sins and false gods they follow. There is still hope if sinners ask God for help and feel sorry for both their hidden and obvious wrongs. 

True regret includes understanding that nothing can save us except God. The people admitted they deserved punishment but asked God not to give them what they deserve. We need to accept God’s fairness while hoping for His kindness.

Real sorrow goes beyond just feeling bad about sin; it means turning away from sin completely. Just as a loving parent feels sad when their child misbehaves, God feels hurt by His people’s actions. Mercy always comes from Him when asked.

So, let scared sinners and those who’ve strayed stop worrying about God’s plans or past failures. Instead, they should trust in God’s mercy, lower themselves before Him, try to get free from evil influences, stay away from sin, work hard at using spiritual tools, and wait patiently for God’s timing. This way, they will surely experience His kindness.

11.o: Men ought not to be blamed for their parentage, so long as they by their personal merits roll away any reproach. God had forgiven Israel, therefore Jephthah will forgive. He speaks not with confidence of his success, knowing how justly God might suffer the Ammonites to prevail for the further punishment of Israel. Nor does he speak with any confidence at all in himself. If he succeed, it is the Lord delivers them into his hand; he thereby reminds his countrymen to look up to God as the Giver of victory. The same question as here, in fact, is put to those who desire salvation by Christ. If he save you, will ye be willing that he shall rule you? On no other terms will he save you. If he make you happy, shall he make you holy? If he be your helper, shall he be your Head? Jephthah, to obtain a little worldly honour, was willing to expose his life: shall we be discouraged in our Christian warfare by the difficulties we may meet with, when Christ has promised a crown of life to him that overcometh?
11.m:
People shouldn’t be judged based on their family background if they have done good things themselves. Since God forgave Israel, Jephthah will forgive too. 

He doesn’t talk about winning confidently because God could let the Ammonites win to punish Israel even more. And he’s not confident in himself either. If he wins, it’s only because God helps him. This reminds everyone to thank God for giving them victory.

This is like asking people who want Jesus to save them: Will you accept Jesus’ rule if he saves you? There are no other conditions. If he makes you happy, will he make you holy? If he helps you, will he lead you?

Jephthah risked his life to get some honor. Shouldn’t Christians face challenges bravely since Jesus promises rewards to those who overcome?

11.o: One instance of the honour and respect we owe to God, as our God, is, rightly to employ what he gives us to possess. Receive it from him, use it for him, and part with it when he calls for it. The whole of this message shows that Jephthah was well acquainted with the books of Moses. His argument was clear, and his demand reasonable. Those who possess the most courageous faith, will be the most disposed for peace, and the readiest to make advances to obtain; but rapacity and ambition often cloak their designs under a plea of equity, and render peaceful endeavours of no avail.
11.m:
We should honor God by using what he gives us properly. We get everything from God, so we should use it for him and give it back when he asks.

Jephthah knew the teachings of Moses very well. He made a strong case and asked fairly.

People with real faith want peace and are ready to work toward it. But greedy and ambitious people often pretend they are fair, making peace efforts useless.

11.o: Several important lessons are to be learned from Jephthah's vow. 1. There may be remainders of distrust and doubting, even in the hearts of true and great believers. 2. Our vows to God should not be as a purchase of the favour we desire, but to express gratitude to him. 3. We need to be very well-advised in making vows, lest we entangle ourselves. 4. What we have solemnly vowed to God, we must perform, if it be possible and lawful, though it be difficult and grievous to us. 5. It well becomes children, obediently and cheerfully to submit to their parents in the Lord. It is hard to say what Jephthah did in performance of his vow; but it is thought that he did not offer his daughter as a burnt-offering. Such a sacrifice would have been an abomination to the Lord; it is supposed she was obliged to remain unmarried, and apart from her family. Concerning this and some other such passages in the sacred history, about which learned men are divided and in doubt, we need not perplex ourselves; what is necessary to our salvation, thanks be to God, is plain enough. If the reader recollects the promise of Christ concerning the teaching of the Holy Spirit, and places himself under this heavenly Teacher, the Holy Ghost will guide to all truth in every passage, so far as it is needful to be understood.
11.m:
Several important lessons come from Jephthah's vow. 

First, even strong believers might still have doubts sometimes. 

Second, our promises to God shouldn't be to earn his favor, but to show our thankfulness. 

Third, we should think carefully before making vows because they can trap us. 

Fourth, once we make a serious promise to God, we must keep it if it’s legal and right, even if it’s tough for us. 

Fifth, children should obey and follow their parents’ wishes respectfully. 

It's unclear exactly what Jephthah did to fulfill his vow. But many believe he didn’t sacrifice his daughter as a burnt offering, since that would have been against God’s law. Instead, she likely had to live alone without getting married. 

There are some parts of the Bible where scholars disagree on the details. But don’t worry too much about those confusing bits. The essential truths for our faith are clearly stated. 

If you remember Jesus' promise about the Holy Spirit guiding us, and trust in that guidance, the Holy Spirit will help you understand everything you truly need to know.

12.o: The Ephraimites had the same quarrel with Jephthah as with Gideon. Pride was at the bottom of the quarrel; only by that comes contention. It is ill to fasten names of reproach upon persons or countries, as is common, especially upon those under outward disadvantages. It often occasions quarrels that prove of ill consequence, as it did here. No contentions are so bitter as those between brethren or rivals for honour. What need we have to watch and pray against evil tempers! May the Lord incline all his people to follow after things which make for peace!
12.m:
The Ephraimites were upset with Jephthah just like they were with Gideon. The problem started because of pride. When people get proud, arguments happen.

It’s wrong to call others mean names, especially if they don’t have much money or power. This kind of name-calling can cause fights that lead to bad results. 

Fights among brothers or people who want the same honor are the worst kinds. We really need to be careful about our attitudes. Let’s hope God helps everyone stay peaceful and kind to each other.

12.o: We have here a short account of three more of the judges of Israel. The happiest life of individuals, and the happiest state of society, is that which affords the fewest remarkable events. To live in credit and quiet, to be peacefully useful to those around us, to possess a clear conscience; but, above all, and without which nothing can avail, to enjoy communion with God our Saviour while we live, and to die at peace with God and man, form the substance of all that a wise man can desire.
12.m: Here is a short account of three more judges of Israel. The best life for an individual and the best state for society is one with the least number of big events. Living a peaceful and helpful life, having a clear conscience, and most importantly, enjoying fellowship with God while alive and dying at peace with God and others—these are what a wise person truly desires.

13.o: Israel did evil: then God delivered them again into the hands of the Philistines. When Israel was in this distress, Samson was born. His parents had been long childless. Many eminent persons were born of such mothers. Mercies long waited for, often prove signal mercies; and by them others may be encouraged to continue their hope in God's mercy. The angel notices her affliction. God often sends comfort to his people very seasonably, when they feel their troubles most. This deliverer of Israel must be devoted to God. Manoah's wife was satisfied that the messenger was of God. She gave her husband a particular account, both of the promise and of the precept. Husbands and wives should tell each other their experiences of communion with God, and their improvements in acquaintance with him, that they may help each other in the way that is holy.
13.m:
Israel did bad things, so God let the Philistines trouble them again. During this hard time, Samson was born. His parents had wanted a child for a long time. Many important people came from parents who couldn't have children for years. Long-awaited blessings often turn out to be special ones. Others can find hope in these miracles too. 

The angel mentioned her pain. God often brings comfort right when we need it most. This man chosen to save Israel would belong to God. Manoah’s wife knew the visitor was from God. She told her husband about the promise and the rules. 

Husbands and wives should share what they learn from God and how they grow closer to Him. They can support each other on the path of holiness.

13.o: Blessed are those who have not seen, and yet, as Manoah, have believed. Good men are more careful and desirous to know the duty to be done by them, than to know the events concerning them: duty is ours, events are God's. God will guide those by his counsel, who desire to know their duty, and apply to him to teach them. Pious parents, especially, will beg Divine assistance. The angel repeats the directions he had before given. There is need of much care for the right ordering both of ourselves and our children, that we may be duly separate from the world, and living sacrifices to the Lord.
13.m:
Those who believe without seeing are blessed, just like Manoah did. Good people want to do what’s right more than they want to know what will happen to them. Our responsibility is to do our duty, while God handles the results. 

God guides us if we ask him about our duties and seek his help. Faithful parents often pray for God’s help. The angel gives instructions again, just like before. We need to take great care in managing ourselves and our children so we can stay separate from the world and offer ourselves as living sacrifices to the Lord.

13.o: What Manoah asked for instruction in his duty, he was readily told; but what he asked to gratify his curiosity, was denied. God has in his word given full directions concerning our duty, but never designed to answer other questionings. There are secret things which belong not to us, of which we must be quite contented to be ignorant, while in this world. The name of our Lord is wonderful and secret; but by his wonderful works he makes himself known as far as is needful for us. Prayer is the ascent of the soul to God. But without Christ in the heart by faith, our services are offensive smoke; in him, acceptable flame. We may apply this to Christ's sacrifice of himself for us; he ascended in the flame of his own offering, for by his own blood he entered in once into the holy place, Heb 9:12. In Manoah's reflections there is great fear; We shall surely die. In his wife's reflection there is great faith. As a help meet for him, she encouraged him. Let believers who have had communion with God in the word and prayer, to whom he has graciously manifested himself, and who have had reason to think God has accepted their works, take encouragement from thence in a cloudy and dark day. God would not have done what he has done for my soul, if he had designed to forsake me, and leave me to perish at last; for his work is perfect. Learn to reason as Manoah's wife; If God designed me to perish under his wrath, he would not give me tokens of his favour.
13.m:
Manoah asked for guidance on how to do his duty, and he got an answer. But when he asked about things just out of curiosity, he was told no. God’s word gives us all the instructions we need about what to do, but He didn’t intend to answer questions beyond that.

There are some secrets that don't concern us, and we should be happy being unaware of them here on earth. God’s name is mysterious and hidden, yet through His amazing acts, He shows Himself enough so we know what we need to know.

Prayer is reaching up to God. But without Jesus in our hearts by faith, our prayers are like bad-smelling smoke. With Jesus, they’re like pleasant flames.

We could compare this to Christ’s sacrifice for us. He rose in the fire of His own offering. By His own blood, He went into the holy place once, Hebrews 9:12 tells us.

In Manoah’s thoughts, he felt scared: “We will surely die.” But his wife believed more strongly. She said, “God gave you signs of His favor, so keep going.”

When people have met with God in His word and prayer, and feel He has shown kindness to them, let them find comfort even on days when things seem hard. If God did good things for me before, He won’t suddenly abandon me now. His work is complete.

Learn to think like Manoah’s wife: If God meant to punish me, He wouldn’t show me kindness.

13.o: The Spirit of the Lord began to move Samson when a youth. This was evidence that the Lord blessed him. Where God gives his blessing, he gives his Spirit to qualify for the blessing. Those are blessed indeed in whom the Spirit of grace begins to work in the days of their childhood. Samson drank no wine or strong drink, yet excelled in strength and courage, for he had the Spirit of God moving him; therefore be not drunk with wine, but be filled with the Spirit.
13.m:
When Samson was young, the Spirit of the Lord started working in him. This showed that God blessed him. When God blesses someone, he also gives them his Spirit to help them. People are truly blessed if the Spirit of grace starts working in them during their childhood. 

Samson didn’t drink wine or other strong drinks, yet he became very strong and brave because the Spirit of God was moving within him. So don’t get drunk on wine, but let yourself be filled with the Spirit instead.

14.o: As far as Samson's marriage was a common case, it was weak and foolish of him to set his affections upon a daughter of the Philistines. Shall one, not only an Israelite, but a Nazarite, devoted to the Lord, covet to become one with a worshipper of Dagon? It does not appear that he had any reason to think her wise or virtuous, or any way likely to be a help meet for him; but he saw something in her agreeable to his fancy. He that, in the choice of a wife, is only guided by his eye, and governed by his fancy, must afterwards thank himself if he find a Philistine in his arms. Yet it was well done not to proceed till Samson had made his parents acquainted with the matter. Children ought not to marry, nor to move towards it, without the advice and consent of their parents. Samson's parents did well to dissuade him from yoking himself unequally with unbelievers. It seems that it pleased God to leave Samson to follow his own inclinations, intending to bring out good from his conduct; and his parents consented, because he was bent upon it. However, his example is not recorded for us to do likewise.
14.m:
Samson’s decision to marry a Philistine woman was unwise. As an Israelite and a Nazirite dedicated to God, shouldn’t he have wanted to marry someone who worships the true God instead of Dagon?

There’s no evidence she was smart or good. But Samson liked how she looked. When choosing a wife, if you just go by looks and feelings, you might end up regretting it later.

It was right of Samson to tell his parents about his plans first. Kids should talk to their parents before getting married. His parents tried to stop him from marrying a Philistine, which was a good thing.

God allowed Samson to follow his desires, planning to use it for good purposes. Even though his parents agreed, we shouldn’t copy what Samson did.

14.o: By enabling him to kill a lion, God let Samson know what he could do in the strength of the Spirit of the Lord, that he might never be afraid to look the greatest difficulties in the face. He was alone in the vineyards, whither he had rambled. Young people consider not how they exposed themselves to the roaring lion that seeks to devour, when they wander from their prudent, pious parents. Nor do men consider what lions lurk in the vineyards, the vineyards of red wines. Our Lord Jesus having conquered Satan, that roaring lion, believers, like Samson, find honey in the carcass abundant strength and satisfaction, enough for themselves, and for all their friends.
14.m:
God showed Samson he could defeat a lion by giving him strength from the Spirit of the Lord. This helped Samson feel brave enough to face any challenge. 

Samson was alone in some vineyards where he shouldn’t have been. When young people leave the care of wise, godly parents, they put themselves at risk. They don't see the dangers around them, like the lion ready to attack. 

Adults often don’t realize the hidden dangers too. But after Jesus defeated Satan—the big danger—believers found plenty of strength and joy, just like Samson did. They had enough to help themselves and others.

14.o: Samson's riddle literally meant no more than that he had got honey, for food and for pleasure, from the lion, which in its strength and fury was ready to devour him. But the victory of Christ over Satan, by means of his humiliation, agonies, and death, and the exaltation that followed to him, with the glory thence to the Father, and spiritual advantages to his people, seem directly alluded to. And even death, that devouring monster, being robbed of his sting, and stripped of his horror, forwards the soul to the realms of bliss. In these and other senses, out of the eater comes forth meat, and out of the strong, sweetness. Samson's companions obliged his wife to get the explanation from him. A worldly wife, or a worldly friend, is to a godly man as an enemy in the camp, who will watch every opportunity to betray him. No union can be comfortable or lasting, where secrets cannot be intrusted, without danger of being divulged. Satan, in his temptations, could not do us the mischief he does, if he did not plough with the heifer of our corrupt nature. His chief advantage against us arises from his correspondence with our deceitful hearts and inbred lusts. This proved an occasion of weaning Samson from his new relations. It were well for us, if the unkindness we meet with from the world, and our disappointments in it, obliged us by faith and prayer to return to our heavenly Father's house, and to rest there. See how little confidence is to be put in man. Whatever pretence of friendship may be made, a real Philistine will soon be weary of a true Israelite.
14.m:
Samson’s riddle simply meant that he found honey inside a dead lion, which once tried to eat him. But it also points to Jesus’ victory over Satan through his suffering and death, leading to his glorification and blessings for God’s people. Even death, usually scary, now helps souls reach heaven. 

In different ways, good things come from difficult situations. Samson’s friends forced his wife to find out what the riddle meant. Having a worldly wife or friend can be harmful, like having an enemy in the camp who might reveal secrets. Trust cannot grow when you have to hide everything.

Satan uses our weaknesses to hurt us. He works best when he connects with our sinful desires. This situation caused Samson to distance himself from his new family. We should use the troubles we face to draw closer to God instead. Don’t trust people too much. Even those who claim to be friendly might turn against someone truly faithful.

15.o: When there are differences between relations, let those be reckoned the wisest and best, who are most forward to forgive or forget, and most willing to stoop and yield for the sake of peace. In the means which Samson employed, we must look at the power of God supplying them, and making them successful, to mortify the pride and punish the wickedness of the Philistines. The Philistines threatened Samson's wife that they would burn her and her father's house. She, to save herself and oblige her countrymen, betrayed her husband; and the very thing that she feared, and by sin sought to avoid, came upon her! She, and her father's house, were burnt with fire, and by her countrymen, whom she thought to oblige by the wrong she did to her husband. The mischief we seek to escape by any unlawful practices, we often pull down upon our own heads.
15.m:
